words.description: これ (kore) means 'this'. It is used to point to something that is near the speaker. Beginners often use this word when showing or talking about an object close to them, like a book or phone. In many sentences, the particle は (wa) marks the topic of the sentence, showing what we are talking about. For example, in これは本です, は tells us that 'this' is the topic being explained.

example_sentences.romaji: Kore wa watashi no pen desu.
example_sentences.telugu_pronunciation: కొరే వా వతషి నో పెన్ దేశ్

words.description: それ (sore) means 'that'. It is used for objects that are near the listener but not near the speaker. Beginners often use it when asking about something another person has. The particle は marks the topic of the sentence and tells us what is being explained. Using それ helps learners practice basic pointing words in Japanese conversations.

example_sentences.romaji: Sore wa watashi no hon desu.
example_sentences.telugu_pronunciation: సొరే వా వతషి నో హోన్ దేశ్

words.english_meaning: That (far from both speaker and listener)
words.description: あれ (are) means 'that over there'. It is used when the object is far away from both the speaker and the listener. Beginners use it when pointing to something at a distance like a mountain or building. The particle は marks the topic of the sentence, showing what we are talking about. This word is part of the Japanese 'ko-so-a-do' system used for pointing.

example_sentences.english_meaning: That over there is a mountain.
That over there is a school.
That over there is a car.

words.description: です (desu) is a polite verb used at the end of sentences. It means 'is', 'am', or 'are' in English depending on the sentence. Beginners use it in almost every polite statement in Japanese. It usually comes after nouns or adjectives to complete a polite sentence. The particle は often appears earlier in the sentence to mark the topic before ending with です.

は is a very important Japanese particle. Although the hiragana is 'ha', it is pronounced 'wa' when used as a particle. It marks the topic of the sentence, meaning the thing we are talking about. Beginners should think of it as 'as for...' or 'about...'. After marking the topic, the rest of the sentence explains something about that topic.

か (ka) is a Japanese particle used to make a sentence into a question. It is usually placed at the end of a sentence. Beginners should remember that Japanese questions do not need a change in word order like English; simply adding か makes it a question. For example, in これは本ですか, the particle か turns the statement into the question 'Is this a book?'. The particle は earlier in the sentence marks the topic, and か indicates that the speaker is asking something.

わたし (watashi) means 'I' or 'me'. It is a common and polite way to refer to yourself in Japanese conversations. Beginners will often start sentences with わたし when introducing themselves. The particle は usually follows わたし to mark the topic, meaning the sentence is about the speaker. This makes it easy to form simple sentences like 'I am a student'.

あなた (anata) means 'you'. It is used to refer to the person you are speaking to. In Japanese conversations, people often avoid using 'you' and instead use the person's name, but beginners still learn this word early. The particle は can follow あなた to mark the topic of the sentence. This allows simple sentences like 'You are a student'.

example_sentences.sentence: あなたは日本人ですか。
あなたは水を飲みます。

words.description: はい (hai) means 'yes'. It is used to give a positive answer or agreement in conversation. Beginners will hear this word very often in daily Japanese communication. It can be used alone or before answering a question. In polite conversation, it shows acknowledgment or confirmation.

いいえ (iie) means 'no'. It is used to give a negative answer to a question. Beginners will use it when they want to politely disagree or say something is not correct. Often it is followed by a full sentence explaining the correct information. In polite conversation, it sounds softer than simply saying 'no'.

example_sentences.sentence: いいえ、学生ではありません。
example_sentences.english_meaning: No, I am not a student.

example_sentences.sentence: いいえ、それは水ではありません。
example_sentences.romaji: Iie, sore wa mizu dewa arimasen.
example_sentences.telugu_pronunciation: ఈఏ సొరే వా మిజు దేవా అరిమాసేన్

words.description: ありがとう (arigatou) means 'thank you'. It is used to express gratitude when someone helps you or does something kind. Beginners will hear and use this word very often in daily Japanese conversations. The more polite form is ありがとうございます, which is used in formal situations. This word usually appears alone and does not require particles like は or を because it functions as a complete expression of gratitude.

すみません (sumimasen) means 'excuse me' or 'sorry'. It is used when apologizing, getting someone's attention, or politely asking for help. Beginners will hear this word frequently in daily life, such as in shops or when asking questions. It is a polite expression and can be used in many situations. This word normally appears as a complete expression and does not need particles like は or を.

example_sentences.sentence: すみません、水をください。
example_sentences.english_meaning: Excuse me, please give me water.

example_sentences.sentence: すみません、これは何ですか。
example_sentences.romaji: Sumimasen, kore wa nan desu ka.
example_sentences.telugu_pronunciation: సుమిమసేన్ కొరే వా నాన్ దేశ్ కా

words.description: おはよう (ohayou) means 'good morning'. It is used when greeting someone in the morning. Beginners will often use the more polite form おはようございます in formal situations. This word is commonly used when meeting friends, family, or coworkers in the morning. It usually stands alone as a greeting and does not need particles like は or を.

こんにちは (konnichiwa) is a common Japanese greeting meaning 'hello' or 'good afternoon'. It is used during the daytime when meeting someone. Beginners will use this word frequently when greeting people politely. Although it is written with は at the end, the pronunciation is 'wa'. This greeting usually appears by itself and does not require particles because it functions as a complete greeting.

みず (mizu) means 'water'. It is a very common word used in daily life when talking about drinking water or water in general. Beginners will often use this word in simple sentences. The particle を is often used with みず to show that water is the object of an action like drinking. For example, 水を飲みます means 'drink water', where を marks the object receiving the action.

ごはん (gohan) means 'rice' or 'meal'. In Japanese daily life, it often refers to cooked rice or a general meal like breakfast or dinner. Beginners will hear this word very frequently because rice is a common food in Japan. The particle を is often used with ごはん when it is the object of an action like eating. For example, ごはんを食べます means 'eat rice', where を marks rice as the object receiving the action.

example_sentences.sentence: 私はごはんを食べます。

words.description: すし (sushi) is a famous Japanese food made with rice and often fish or vegetables. It is one of the most well-known Japanese dishes around the world. Beginners often learn this word early when studying Japanese culture and food. The particle を is commonly used when sushi is the object of an action such as eating. For example, すしを食べます means 'eat sushi'.

おちゃ (ocha) means 'tea', especially Japanese green tea. It is a very common drink in Japan and is often served with meals. Beginners will see this word often when learning about Japanese food culture. The particle を can mark tea as the object when someone drinks it. For example, おちゃを飲みます means 'drink tea', where を marks tea as the object of the action.

example_sentences.sentence: 私はおちゃを飲みます。

words.description: べんごし (bengoshi) means 'lawyer'. A lawyer is a professional who helps people with legal matters and represents them in court. Beginners learn this word when studying professions in Japanese. The particle は often follows a person to mark the topic when describing someone's job. For example, 彼は弁護士です means 'He is a lawyer', where は marks the topic.

example_sentences.sentence: あなたは弁護士ですか。

words.description: せんせい (sensei) means 'teacher' or 'master'. It is used to address teachers, doctors, and other respected professionals. Beginners will use this word often in school or learning environments. The particle は may follow a person when describing that someone is a teacher. For example, 先生です means 'is a teacher', where です completes the polite sentence.

がくせい (gakusei) means 'student'. It refers to a person who studies at a school, college, or university. Beginners often use this word when introducing themselves in Japanese. The particle は is commonly used after a person to mark the topic when describing their role or identity. For example, 私は学生です means 'I am a student', where は marks 'I' as the topic and です completes the polite sentence.

いしゃ (isha) means 'doctor'. It refers to a medical professional who treats sick or injured people. Beginners learn this word when studying professions in Japanese. The particle は is often used to mark the topic when describing someone's job. For example, 彼は医者です means 'He is a doctor', where は marks the topic and です finishes the polite statement.

かっこいい (kakkoii) means 'cool', 'handsome', or 'stylish'. It is used to describe a person, object, or action that looks impressive or attractive. Beginners often use this adjective when talking about people, clothes, or things they like. The particle が is sometimes used to mark the subject being described. For example, 彼がかっこいいです means 'He is cool', where が marks the subject.

example_sentences.sentence: この車はかっこいいです。
先生はかっこいいです。

words.description: たべます (tabemasu) means 'to eat' in polite Japanese form. It is used when talking about eating food. Beginners use this verb very often when learning daily activities in Japanese. The particle を is used before the food to mark it as the object of the action. For example, ごはんを食べます means 'eat rice', where を shows that rice is the thing being eaten.

example_sentences.sentence: 学生はごはんを食べます。

words.description: のみます (nomimasu) means 'to drink'. It is used when talking about drinking water, tea, or other drinks. Beginners commonly learn this verb together with words like water or tea. The particle を marks the drink as the object of the action. For example, 水を飲みます means 'drink water', where を shows that water is the thing being drunk.

example_sentences.sentence: 私はおちゃを飲みます。

words.description: 母 (はは / haha) means 'my mother' when speaking about your own mother to other people. It is commonly used in conversations when you introduce or talk about your family. Beginners should remember that Japanese often has different words for 'my mother' and 'someone else's mother'. In example sentences, the particle は marks the topic of the sentence. The particle が marks the subject of the sentence, meaning the person doing or being something.

example_sentences.sentence: 母がごはんを作ります。

words.description: お父さん (おとうさん / otousan) means father or dad. It is used politely when talking about someone else's father or when calling your own father respectfully. In daily life, children often call their father 'otousan'. In sentences, the particle は marks the topic being discussed. The particle に is often used to show direction or location.

example_sentences.sentence: お父さんと学校へ行きます。
example_sentences.english_meaning: I go to school with my father.

words.description: お母さん (おかあさん / okaasan) means mother or mom. It is used politely and is common when calling your mother or referring to someone else's mother. In daily Japanese families, children often say 'okaasan'. In sentences, は marks the topic that we are talking about. The particle を marks the object receiving the action.

example_sentences.sentence: お母さんは家にいます。
お母さんがごはんを作ります。

words.description: 家 (いえ / ie) means house or home. It refers to a place where people live with their family. Beginners often see this word in daily life conversations when talking about where someone lives. The particle に is used to show location or direction. The particle で is used to show where an action happens.

example_sentences.sentence: 家でごはんを食べます。

words.description: 学校 (がっこう / gakkou) means school. It is the place where students go to study and learn. This is a very common word for beginners learning Japanese. The particle に is used to show direction when someone goes to school. The particle で is used when an action happens at school.

友達 (ともだち / tomodachi) means friend. It refers to a person you like, talk with, and spend time with. Beginners often use this word when talking about people they know or meet. In Japanese sentences, the particle は marks the topic we are talking about. The particle と means 'with' and is used when doing something together with a friend.

example_sentences.sentence: 友達と学校に行きます。
example_sentences.english_meaning: I go to school with my friend.

words.description: どこ (doko) means 'where'. It is a question word used to ask about a place or location. Beginners use it to ask where something or someone is. In Japanese questions, the particle は marks the topic being asked about. The particle に can mark location or direction.

example_sentences.sentence: あなたの家はどこですか。
example_sentences.romaji: Anata no ie wa doko desu ka.
example_sentences.telugu_pronunciation: అనత నో ఇఎ వా దొకొ దేశ్ కా

words.description: 誰 (だれ / dare) means 'who'. It is used to ask about a person's identity. Beginners use this word when asking about people in conversations. The particle は marks the topic of the sentence. The particle が marks the subject doing the action.

example_sentences.romaji: Ano hito wa dare desu ka.
example_sentences.telugu_pronunciation: అనో హితో వా దరె దేశ్ కా

example_sentences.romaji: Kore wa dare no hon desu ka.
example_sentences.telugu_pronunciation: కొరే వా దరె నో హోన్ దేశ్ కా

words.description: 何 (なに / nani) means 'what'. It is used to ask about things, objects, or actions. Beginners often use this word when asking questions about something unknown. The particle を marks the object receiving the action. The particle は marks the topic of the sentence.

example_sentences.sentence: あなたは何を飲みますか。
example_sentences.romaji: Anata wa nani o nomimasu ka.
example_sentences.telugu_pronunciation: అనత వా నని ఓ నొమిమాస్ కా

words.description: 今 (いま / ima) means 'now' or the present moment. It is used when talking about something happening at the current time. Beginners often use it when describing what they are doing now. The particle は marks the topic of the sentence. Time words like 今 usually come at the beginning of the sentence.

今日 (きょう / kyou) means 'today'. It refers to the current day. Beginners use it when talking about plans or events happening today. Time words like 今日 usually appear at the beginning of sentences. The particle は can mark today's topic in a sentence.

example_sentences.sentence: 今日は学校に行きます。
今日は友達と話します。
example_sentences.english_meaning: I talk with my friend today.
example_sentences.telugu_meaning: నేను ఈ రోజు నా స్నేహితుడితో మాట్లాడుతాను.

words.description: 明日 (あした / ashita) means 'tomorrow'. It refers to the next day after today. Beginners use it when talking about plans or events happening in the future. Time words like 明日 usually appear at the beginning of the sentence. The particle に can mark time when something happens.

を (wo / pronounced 'o') is a Japanese particle used to mark the object of a verb. The object is the thing that receives the action in the sentence. For example, in the sentence ごはんを食べます (I eat rice), ごはん is the object and を shows that the action 'eat' happens to it. Beginners should remember that を is always used before verbs like eat, drink, watch, read, etc. Even though it is written as を, it is pronounced simply as 'o' in modern Japanese.

に (ni) is a very important Japanese particle used to show time, direction, or location. It is commonly used with verbs like 行きます (go), 来ます (come), and います (exist for living things). For example, in 学校に行きます, the particle に shows the destination where someone goes. It can also show time, like 3時に行きます meaning 'go at 3 o'clock'. Beginners should remember that に often shows where something happens or where someone moves.

へ (he but pronounced 'e') is a Japanese particle used to indicate direction. It shows where someone or something is heading. It is often used with movement verbs like 行きます (go) and 来ます (come). For example, 学校へ行きます means 'go toward school'. Beginners should remember that へ focuses on the direction of movement rather than the final destination.

が (ga) is a particle used to mark the subject of a sentence. The subject is the person or thing that performs the action or has the described quality. For example, in the sentence 猫がいます (There is a cat), 猫 is the subject and が marks it. Beginners often see が used when introducing new information or answering questions like 'who'. Compared to は, が focuses more on the subject itself.

の (no) is a particle used to show possession or relationship between two nouns. It works similarly to the English word 'of' or the Telugu word 'యొక్క'. For example, わたしの本 means 'my book'. Beginners will see this particle very often when describing ownership or belonging. It connects two nouns together in a simple way.

も (mo) is a Japanese particle that means 'also' or 'too'. It is used when something is the same as another thing mentioned before. For example, if someone says 'I am a student', another person can say 'I am also a student' using も. This particle replaces は in many sentences when showing similarity. Beginners should remember that も connects ideas that are similar.

example_sentences.english_meaning: My mother is also at home.

words.description: で (de) is a particle used to show where an action happens. It marks the place where a verb activity occurs. For example, 学校で勉強します means 'study at school'. Beginners should remember that に shows location or destination, while で shows where an action takes place. It is often used with verbs like eat, study, work, and play.

example_sentences.sentence: 家でごはんを食べます。

words.description: と (to) is a particle that means 'with' or 'and'. When used between people it means doing something together with someone. It can also connect two nouns meaning 'and'. Beginners will see it often in sentences like 'friend and me' or 'talk with someone'. It is useful for showing relationships between people or things.

example_sentences.sentence: 先生と学校へ行きます。
example_sentences.english_meaning: I go to school with the teacher.

words.description: でも (demo) means 'but' or 'however'. It is used to connect two sentences that contrast each other. Beginners often use it when saying something different from the previous statement. It usually appears at the beginning of a new sentence. It helps show contrast between ideas.

example_sentences.sentence: 今日は忙しいです。でも学校へ行きます。
example_sentences.romaji: Kyou wa isogashii desu. Demo gakkou e ikimasu.
example_sentences.telugu_pronunciation: క్యో వా ఇసొగషి దేశ్. దెమొ గక్కో ఏ ఇకిమాస్
example_sentences.english_meaning: Today I am busy. But I go to school.
example_sentences.telugu_meaning: ఈ రోజు నేను బిజీగా ఉన్నాను. కానీ పాఠశాలకు వెళ్తాను.

example_sentences.english_meaning: It is raining. But I go.

words.description: そして (soshite) means 'and' or 'and then'. It connects two related sentences or events in order. Beginners often use it when telling a story or describing steps. It usually appears at the beginning of a new sentence. It helps continue the flow of ideas.

example_sentences.sentence: 学校へ行きます。そして勉強します。
example_sentences.romaji: Gakkou e ikimasu. Soshite benkyou shimasu.
example_sentences.telugu_pronunciation: గక్కో ఏ ఇకిమాస్. సొషితె బెంక్యో షిమాస్
example_sentences.english_meaning: I go to school. And then I study.

example_sentences.sentence: 水を飲みます。そしてごはんを食べます。
example_sentences.romaji: Mizu o nomimasu. Soshite gohan o tabemasu.
example_sentences.telugu_pronunciation: మిజు ఓ నొమిమాస్. సొషితె గొహన్ ఓ తబేమాస్
example_sentences.english_meaning: I drink water. Then I eat food.
example_sentences.telugu_meaning: నేను నీరు తాగుతాను. తరువాత భోజనం తింటాను.

words.description: だから (dakara) means 'therefore' or 'so'. It is used to show the reason or result of something. Beginners use it when explaining why something happens. It usually comes at the beginning of the second sentence. It connects cause and result in a simple way.

example_sentences.sentence: 今日は忙しいです。だから行きません。
example_sentences.romaji: Kyou wa isogashii desu. Dakara ikimasen.
example_sentences.telugu_pronunciation: క్యో వా ఇసొగషి దేశ్. దకర ఇకిమాసెన్
example_sentences.english_meaning: Today I am busy. So I do not go.
example_sentences.telugu_meaning: ఈ రోజు నేను బిజీగా ఉన్నాను. కాబట్టి నేను వెళ్లను.

example_sentences.sentence: 雨です。だから家にいます。
example_sentences.romaji: Ame desu. Dakara ie ni imasu.
example_sentences.telugu_pronunciation: అమె దేశ్. దకర ఇఎ ని ఇమాస్
example_sentences.english_meaning: It is raining. Therefore I stay at home.
example_sentences.telugu_meaning: వర్షం పడుతోంది. అందువల్ల నేను ఇంట్లో ఉంటాను.

words.description: 忙しい (いそがしい / isogashii) means 'busy'. It describes a person who has a lot of work or many things to do. Beginners often use it to talk about their daily schedule. In sentences the particle は marks the topic. It is commonly used with です in polite speech.

暇 (ひま / hima) means free time or not busy. It describes when someone has nothing to do. Beginners use it when talking about their free time. In sentences the particle は marks the topic being discussed. It is commonly used with です.

いつ (itsu) means 'when'. It is a question word used to ask about time. Beginners use it to ask when something happens. In questions the particle は may mark the topic. The sentence usually ends with か to show it is a question.

example_sentences.sentence: あなたはいつ来ますか。

words.description: 朝 (あさ / asa) means morning. It refers to the early part of the day after waking up and before noon. Beginners will often use this word when talking about daily routines like waking up, eating breakfast, or going to school. In sentences like 私は朝に起きます, the particle は marks the topic of the sentence, and に marks the time when the action happens.

example_sentences.english_meaning: I wake up in the morning.
I go to school in the morning.

words.description: 昼 (ひる / hiru) means afternoon or noon. It refers to the middle of the day when people usually eat lunch. Beginners often use this word when talking about lunch time or daily schedules. In sentences like 昼にごはんを食べます, the particle に shows the time of the action and を marks the object receiving the action.

example_sentences.sentence: 昼にごはんを食べます。

example_sentences.english_meaning: I talk with my friend at noon.

words.description: 夜 (よる / yoru) means night. It refers to the time after evening when people usually rest or sleep. Beginners use this word to describe activities like eating dinner or sleeping. In sentences like 夜に寝ます, the particle に shows the time when the action happens.

example_sentences.english_meaning: I read a book at night.

words.description: こんばんは (konbanwa) means good evening. It is a greeting used in the evening when meeting someone. Beginners should use this greeting politely when talking to people in the evening. Even though it is written with は, it is pronounced 'wa' because it acts as a greeting particle historically.

example_sentences.sentence: 友達にこんばんはと言います。
example_sentences.english_meaning: I say good evening to my friend.

words.description: 毎日 (まいにち / mainichi) means every day. It describes something that happens daily. Beginners use this word when explaining daily habits or routines. In sentences like 私は毎日働きます, the particle は marks the topic of the sentence.

example_sentences.sentence: 毎日日本語を勉強します。
example_sentences.telugu_pronunciation: మైని చి నిహొంగో ఓ బెంక్యో షిమస్

words.description: 後で (あとで / atode) means 'later'. It is used when you plan to do something after some time. Beginners use it to talk about future actions happening soon. In sentences like 後で友達と話します, the particle と means 'with' and shows who you do the action with.

example_sentences.english_meaning: I talk with my friend later.

example_sentences.sentence: 後でごはんを食べます。

words.description: 早い (はやい / hayai) means early or fast. It can describe time or speed. Beginners often use it when talking about waking up early or something happening quickly. In sentences like 朝は早いです, the particle は marks the topic being described.

遅い (おそい / osoi) means late or slow. It is the opposite of 早い. Beginners use it when something happens later than expected or moves slowly. In sentences like 電車は遅いです, the particle は marks the topic of the sentence.

起きます (おきます / okimasu) means 'to wake up' or 'to get up'. It is commonly used when talking about waking up in the morning. Beginners often use it to describe their daily routine. In sentences like 毎朝6時に起きます, the particle に shows the time when the action happens.

example_sentences.romaji: Watashi wa asa rokuji ni okimasu.
example_sentences.telugu_pronunciation: వతషి వా అసా రొకుజి ని ఒకిమస్

example_sentences.english_meaning: I wake up at 7 every morning.

words.description: 寝ます (ねます / nemasu) means 'to sleep' or 'to go to bed'. It is used when talking about sleeping at night or taking a nap. Beginners use it to describe their daily routine. In sentences like 夜10時に寝ます, the particle に shows the time when the action happens.

example_sentences.romaji: Watashi wa yoru juuji ni nemasu.
example_sentences.telugu_pronunciation: వతషి వా యోరు జూజి ని నేమస్
example_sentences.english_meaning: I go to bed at 10 PM.
I sleep at 11 PM every night.

words.description: 働きます (はたらきます / hatarakimasu) means 'to work'. It is commonly used when talking about going to a job or performing tasks. Beginners use it to describe their daily work routine. In sentences like 毎日働きます, は marks the topic of the sentence.

example_sentences.english_meaning: My father works at the company.

words.english_meaning: rest / take a break / be absent
words.description: 休みます (やすみます / yasumimasu) means 'to rest' or 'take a break'. It is used when someone is resting, taking leave from work, or taking a day off. Beginners can use it to talk about relaxing or not going to work or school. In sentences like 今日は休みます, は marks the topic of the sentence.

始まります (はじまります / hajimarimasu) means 'to begin' or 'to start'. It is used when an event, class, or activity begins. Beginners use it when describing the start of lessons, meetings, or events. In sentences like 授業は9時に始まります, は marks the topic and に shows the time of the event.

example_sentences.sentence: 授業は9時に始まります。
会議は午後3時に始まります。
example_sentences.romaji: Kaigi wa gogo sanji ni hajimarimasu.
example_sentences.telugu_pronunciation: కైగి వా గొగో సాంజి ని హజిమరిమస్

words.description: 一 (いち / ichi) means the number one in Japanese. It is the first number in counting and is used when counting objects, people, or steps. Beginners often see it in simple counting or when talking about quantity. In sentences, particles like 「は」 mark the topic of the sentence. For example, in 一つはりんごです, 「は」 shows that 'one item' is the topic being explained.

example_sentences.sentence: りんごが一つあります。

words.description: 二 (に / ni) means the number two. It is used when counting two items, people, or objects. Beginners will see it often when counting things in daily life. In sentences, particles like 「が」 can mark the subject of the sentence. This means the counted object is the thing being talked about.

example_sentences.sentence: りんごが二つあります。

words.description: 三 (さん / san) means the number three. It is used when counting three items or people. Beginners often see this number in dates, counting, and basic math. The particle 「が」 can mark the subject in sentences with numbers. This shows what objects are being counted.

example_sentences.sentence: りんごが三つあります。

words.description: 四 (よん / yon) means the number four. It is used when counting four items or people. Beginners should know that four sometimes has another reading 'shi', but 'yon' is commonly used in daily conversation. The particle 「が」 marks the subject when counting objects in a sentence.

example_sentences.sentence: りんごが四つあります。

words.description: 五 (ご / go) means the number five. It is used when counting five objects or people. Beginners will see it in counting, dates, and everyday situations. The particle 「が」 can mark the subject when counting objects. This shows the objects that exist or are being described.

example_sentences.sentence: りんごが五つあります。

words.description: 六 (ろく / roku) means the number six in Japanese. It is used when counting six objects, people, or items. Beginners often see it in daily counting such as six apples or six people. In sentences, the particle 「が」 marks the subject, meaning the thing that exists or is being counted. This helps clearly show what item the number is describing.

example_sentences.sentence: りんごが六つあります。

words.description: 七 (なな / nana) means the number seven in Japanese. It is commonly used when counting seven items or people. Sometimes the reading 'shichi' is also used, but 'nana' is easier and more common in conversation. In sentences, the particle 「が」 marks the subject, meaning the counted objects. This helps learners understand what items are being described.

example_sentences.sentence: りんごが七つあります。

words.description: 八 (はち / hachi) means the number eight in Japanese. It is used when counting eight objects, people, or items. Beginners often see it in basic counting and daily conversation. In example sentences, the particle 「が」 marks the subject, meaning the things being counted. This helps clearly identify the objects that exist.

example_sentences.sentence: りんごが八つあります。

words.description: 九 (きゅう / kyuu) means the number nine in Japanese. It is used when counting nine items or people. Another reading 'ku' exists, but 'kyuu' is commonly used in many situations. The particle 「が」 marks the subject in counting sentences. This shows the objects that exist or are being counted.

example_sentences.sentence: りんごが九つあります。

words.description: 十 (じゅう / juu) means the number ten in Japanese. It is used when counting ten items or describing quantities. Beginners will see it often in numbers like eleven, twelve, and so on. The particle 「が」 can mark the subject in counting sentences, showing the objects that exist. Learning this number helps understand larger Japanese numbers.

十一 (じゅういち / juuichi) means eleven. It is formed by combining 十 (ten) and 一 (one), literally meaning 'ten plus one'. Japanese numbers after ten are usually formed by combining ten with another number. Understanding this pattern helps learners easily understand numbers like twelve, thirteen, and so on.

example_sentences.sentence: りんごが十一あります。

words.description: 十二 (じゅうに / juuni) means twelve. It is formed by combining 十 (ten) and 二 (two), meaning 'ten plus two'. This is part of the basic Japanese counting pattern where numbers after ten are created by adding another number to ten.
十三 (じゅうさん / juusan) means thirteen. It combines 十 (ten) and 三 (three), meaning 'ten plus three'. This number follows the same pattern used for numbers after ten in Japanese counting.

example_sentences.sentence: りんごが十三あります。

words.description: 十四 (じゅうよん / juuyon) means fourteen. It combines 十 (ten) and 四 (four), meaning 'ten plus four'. The reading 'yon' is commonly used instead of 'shi' because it is easier and avoids confusion with words related to death in Japanese.

じ (時 / ji) is used in Japanese to indicate the hour of the day. It is added after numbers to tell time, such as いちじ (1 o'clock) or にじ (2 o'clock). In daily conversation, it helps people talk about schedules, meetings, or activities. In example sentences, the particle 「は」 marks the topic of the sentence, meaning the sentence is talking about that time. The particle 「に」 marks the time when an action happens.

いちじ (一時 / ichiji) means one o'clock. It is formed by combining the number one (いち) and the time marker じ. It is commonly used when talking about schedules or meeting times. In example sentences, 「に」 is used to mark the exact time when an action happens. The particle 「は」 marks the topic of the sentence.

example_sentences.sentence: 一時に昼ごはんを食べます。
example_sentences.english_meaning: I eat lunch at one o'clock.
example_sentences.telugu_meaning: నేను ఒక గంటకు మధ్యాహ్న భోజనం తింటాను.

example_sentences.english_meaning: The meeting is at one o'clock.

words.description: にじ (二時 / niji) means two o'clock. It combines 二 (two) with 時 (hour). It is used when talking about schedules, classes, or appointments. In example sentences, 「に」 marks the time when an action happens. The particle 「は」 marks the topic being discussed in the sentence.

example_sentences.sentence: 二時に学校へ行きます。
example_sentences.english_meaning: I go to school at two o'clock.
The movie is at two o'clock.

words.description: さんじ (三時 / sanji) means three o'clock. It is formed by combining 三 (three) and 時 (hour). Japanese commonly expresses time by placing a number before 時. In sentences, the particle 「に」 marks the specific time when an action happens. The particle 「は」 marks the topic of the sentence when we are talking about a specific time.

example_sentences.english_meaning: I go home at three o'clock.
The meeting is at three o'clock.

words.description: よじ (四時 / yoji) means four o'clock. The number 四 can be read as よん or し, but when telling time it becomes よじ. It is used when talking about daily schedules like classes, work, or meetings. The particle 「に」 marks the time when something happens. The particle 「は」 marks the topic when the sentence is describing a schedule or time.

example_sentences.english_meaning: The shop opens at four o'clock.

words.description: ごじ (五時 / goji) means five o'clock. It is formed by combining 五 (five) and 時 (hour). It is used when talking about daily activities like finishing work or meeting someone. In sentences, 「に」 marks the time when an action happens. The particle 「は」 marks the topic when we talk about schedules.

example_sentences.sentence: 五時に仕事が終わります。

example_sentences.english_meaning: The movie is at five o'clock.

words.description: ろくじ (六時 / rokuji) means six o'clock. It is formed by combining 六 (six) and 時 (hour). This word is commonly used when talking about morning routines or evening schedules. The particle 「に」 marks the exact time when something happens. The particle 「は」 marks the topic when describing a schedule.

example_sentences.english_meaning: I wake up at six o'clock.

words.description: しちじ (七時 / shichiji) means seven o'clock. The number 七 can be read as なな or しち, but when telling time it is commonly read as しちじ. This word is used when talking about schedules such as dinner or meetings. The particle 「に」 marks the time when an action happens. The particle 「は」 marks the topic when describing schedules.

example_sentences.sentence: 七時に夕ごはんを食べます。
example_sentences.english_meaning: I eat dinner at seven o'clock.
example_sentences.telugu_meaning: నేను ఏడు గంటలకు రాత్రి భోజనం తింటాను.

example_sentences.english_meaning: The meeting is at seven o'clock.

words.description: はちじ (八時 / hachiji) means eight o'clock. It is formed by combining 八 (eight) and 時 (hour). This word is commonly used when talking about daily schedules such as school, work, or meals. In Japanese, the particle 「に」 is used to mark the exact time when an action happens. The particle 「は」 marks the topic of the sentence when we are describing a schedule or routine.

example_sentences.sentence: 八時に学校へ行きます。
example_sentences.english_meaning: I go to school at eight o'clock.
The meeting is at eight o'clock.

words.description: くじ (九時 / kuji) means nine o'clock. It is formed by combining 九 (nine) and 時 (hour). This word is used when talking about schedules, appointments, or daily routines. The particle 「に」 marks the exact time when an action happens. The particle 「は」 marks the topic of the sentence when describing time.

example_sentences.sentence: 九時に会社へ行きます。
example_sentences.english_meaning: I go to the office at nine o'clock.
The class is at nine o'clock.

words.description: じゅうじ (十時 / juuji) means ten o'clock. It is formed by combining 十 (ten) and 時 (hour). It is used when talking about time in daily schedules like work, school, or meetings. In example sentences, 「に」 marks the time when an action happens. The particle 「は」 marks the topic of the sentence when describing a schedule.

example_sentences.sentence: 店は十時に閉まります。
example_sentences.english_meaning: The shop closes at ten o'clock.

words.description: いまなんじ (今何時 / ima nanji) means 'what time is it now?'. It is a common question used in daily conversation when asking the current time. 今 means 'now', and 何時 means 'what time'. In example sentences, 「は」 marks the topic of the sentence when asking about the time. This phrase is very useful for beginners when learning to ask about time.

example_sentences.english_meaning: At what time will you go now?

words.description: はん (半 / han) means half. In time expressions, it is used to say 'half past' an hour, such as 三時半 meaning 3:30. It is very common when telling time in daily conversation. In sentences, 「に」 marks the time when an action happens. The particle 「は」 marks the topic when describing schedules.

example_sentences.english_meaning: I wake up at six thirty.
The meeting is at nine thirty.

words.description: ごろ (頃 / goro) means 'around' or 'about' when talking about time. It is used when the exact time is not precise, such as 'around five o'clock'. This word helps speakers give approximate times in conversation. In sentences, 「に」 marks the time when an action happens. The particle 「は」 marks the topic when describing a schedule.

example_sentences.english_meaning: I go home around five o'clock.
example_sentences.telugu_meaning: నేను ఐదు గంటల సమయంలో ఇంటికి వెళ్తాను.

example_sentences.english_meaning: The meeting is around eight o'clock.

words.description: ひゃく (百 / hyaku) means one hundred. It is a basic number used when counting money, people, or objects. In daily life it is often used with yen when talking about prices. In example sentences the particle 「は」 marks the topic of the sentence. When 「に」 appears, it marks the time when an action happens.

example_sentences.sentence: 私は百円を持っています。
example_sentences.romaji: Watashi wa hyaku en o motte imasu.
example_sentences.telugu_pronunciation: వతషి వా హ్యాకు ఎన్ ఓ మోత్తే ఇమాస్

words.description: ごひゃく (五百 / gohyaku) means five hundred. It combines 五 (five) and 百 (hundred). This number is commonly used when talking about prices or counting items. The particle 「は」 marks the topic of the sentence. The particle 「を」 marks the object that receives the action.

example_sentences.romaji: Kono hon wa gohyaku en desu.
example_sentences.telugu_pronunciation: కోనో హోన్ వా గోహ్యాకు ఎన్ దేశ్
example_sentences.telugu_meaning: ఈ పుస్తకం ధర ఐదు వందల యెన్.

words.description: せん (千 / sen) means one thousand. It is used when counting large numbers like money or people. It often appears with yen when talking about prices. The particle 「は」 marks the topic of the sentence. The particle 「が」 marks the subject performing the action.

example_sentences.romaji: Kono tokei wa sen en desu.
example_sentences.telugu_pronunciation: కోనో టోకే వా సెన్ ఎన్ దేశ్

example_sentences.sentence: 私は千円を持っています。
example_sentences.romaji: Watashi wa sen en o motte imasu.
example_sentences.telugu_pronunciation: వతషి వా సెన్ ఎన్ ఓ మోత్తే ఇమాస్

words.description: いちまん (一万 / ichiman) means ten thousand. Japanese numbers group differently compared to English because 万 represents ten thousand. It is often used when talking about large amounts of money. The particle 「は」 marks the topic of the sentence. The particle 「を」 marks the object receiving the action.

example_sentences.sentence: このかばんは一万円です。
example_sentences.romaji: Kono kaban wa ichiman en desu.
example_sentences.telugu_pronunciation: కోనో కబాన్ వా ఇచిమాన్ ఎన్ దేశ్
example_sentences.telugu_meaning: ఈ బ్యాగ్ ధర పది వేల యెన్.

example_sentences.sentence: 私は一万円を持っています。
example_sentences.romaji: Watashi wa ichiman en o motte imasu.
example_sentences.telugu_pronunciation: వతషి వా ఇచిమాన్ ఎన్ ఓ మోత్తే ఇమాస్
example_sentences.telugu_meaning: నా దగ్గర పది వేల యెన్ ఉంది.

words.description: えん (円 / en) means yen, the currency used in Japan. It is used when talking about prices, shopping, and money. Numbers usually come before 円 to show the amount of money. The particle 「は」 marks the topic when describing the price of something. The particle 「を」 marks the money being paid.

example_sentences.romaji: Kono hon wa sen en desu.
example_sentences.telugu_pronunciation: కోనో హోన్ వా సెన్ ఎన్ దేశ్

words.description: ふん (分 / fun) means minute and is used when telling time in minutes. It is often combined with numbers such as 五分 (five minutes) or 十分 (ten minutes). This word is very common when talking about time duration or exact clock time. In example sentences, the particle 「に」 marks the specific time when an action happens. The particle 「は」 marks the topic of the sentence when describing time.

びょう (秒 / byou) means second, the smallest common unit of time. It is used when measuring very short durations such as in sports or precise timing. This word usually appears after numbers like 十秒 (ten seconds). In sentences, 「に」 marks the time when something happens. The particle 「は」 marks the topic of the sentence when describing time.

なんぷん (何分 / nanpun) means 'how many minutes' or 'what minute'. It is used when asking about the minute part of time or duration. For example, someone may ask 何分ですか to ask the minutes of a time. The particle 「か」 is used at the end of a sentence to make a question. In answers, particles like 「に」 may appear to mark the exact time.

example_sentences.english_meaning: At what minute will you come?

words.description: 一分 (いっぷん / ippun) means '1 minute' in Japanese. It is formed by combining the number with 分 (ふん/ぷん), which means 'minute'. Note that the pronunciation of 分 changes depending on the preceding number — it becomes ぷん after some numbers (1, 3, 4, 6, 8, 10) and ふん after others (2, 5, 7, 9). The particle で (de) can be used to say 'in 1 minute' (in ఒక నిమిషం). This word is commonly used when telling time together with 時 (ji / o'clock).

example_sentences.sentence: あと一分で終わります。
example_sentences.english_meaning: It will finish in 1 more minute(s).
I arrive at the station in 1 minute(s).

words.description: 一人 (ひとり / hitori) is used to count people in Japanese. Note that 1 person (ひとり) and 2 people (ふたり) are special native Japanese words, while 3 and above use the Chinese-based readings with にん (nin). The particle が (ga) marks the subject — the people being counted. The particle で (de) after a people counter means 'as a group of' or 'together with that many people'. As a beginner, memorize ひとり and ふたり separately since they are irregular.

example_sentences.sentence: パーティーに一人来ました。

words.description: 二人 (ふたり / futari) is used to count people in Japanese. Note that 1 person (ひとり) and 2 people (ふたり) are special native Japanese words, while 3 and above use the Chinese-based readings with にん (nin). The particle が (ga) marks the subject — the people being counted. The particle で (de) after a people counter means 'as a group of' or 'together with that many people'. As a beginner, memorize ひとり and ふたり separately since they are irregular.

example_sentences.sentence: パーティーに二人来ました。

words.description: 一つ (ひとつ / hitotsu) is a general-purpose counter used for counting objects when no specific counter word applies. The つ (tsu) counter series uses native Japanese words for numbers 1-9, making it unique compared to Chinese-based counters. It can count almost any physical object — fruits, toys, boxes, etc. The particle を (wo) marks the object being counted in an action sentence. The particle が (ga) marks the subject when describing existence.

words.description: 一日 (ついたち / tsuitachi) means the 'first day of the month' in Japanese. The days of the month use special native Japanese readings that must be memorized separately. These are not formed by combining simple number + day, but have their own unique pronunciations. The particle に (ni) is used after date expressions to say 'on' that date. The particle は (wa) marks the topic and is used for general statements.

The particle は (wa) marks the topic — the person whose age is being stated. Note that いっさい (1 year old) and はっさい (8 years old) have slightly different sounds due to sound changes.

words.description: 一階 (いっかい / ikkai) means the '1st floor' of a building in Japanese. 階 (かい/がい) is the counter for floors of buildings. words.description: 一台 (いちだい / ichi-dai) is used to count machines and vehicles in Japanese. 台 (だい / dai) is used for cars, computers, bicycles, televisions, and other mechanical devices. The particle が (ga) marks the subject when describing existence. The particle を (wo) marks the object in action sentences such as 'I bought...' or 'I use...'. 台 does not change its pronunciation much, making it one of the easier counters to learn.

example_sentences.romaji: Chuushajou ni kuruma ga ichi-dai arimasu.

The particle を (wo) marks the object in sentences where something is being done with the flat items. The particle が (ga) marks the subject when describing existence. As a beginner, use this counter whenever you count something thin and flat.

words.description: 一月 (いちがつ / ichi-gatsu) means 'January' in Japanese. Months are formed by combining the number with 月 (がつ / gatsu), which means 'month'. Note: 四月 uses し (shi) instead of よん, and 七月 uses しち (shichi), and 九月 uses く (ku) — these are special readings. The particle に (ni) after a month name means 'in' that month. The particle は (wa) marks the topic when making general statements about the month.

words.description: 五月 (ごがつ / go-gatsu) means 'May' in Japanese. Months are formed by combining the number with 月 (がつ / gatsu), which means 'month'. Note: 四月 uses し (shi) instead of よん, and 七月 uses しち (shichi), and 九月 uses く (ku) — these are special readings. The particle に (ni) after a month name means 'in' that month. The particle は (wa) marks the topic when making general statements about the month.
